title: Dog Ate a Box of Raisins: Emergency Steps and Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog ate raisins, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn why raisin toxicity is dangerous and what you need to do immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Raisin ingestion is a critical medical emergency in dogs that can cause fatal kidney failure even in small amounts. Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately, as symptoms often do not appear for up to 48 hours.

What is raisin toxicity and how does it affect dogs?

Raisins are dried grapes, and both are known to be highly toxic to dogs. Ingestion can lead to acute kidney injury or complete renal failure. While the exact toxic mechanism is still being researched, it is known that even a very small amount can be fatal to certain dogs, regardless of their size or breed.

Is it an emergency if my dog eats raisins?

  • Urgency Level: High. This is a critical medical emergency.
  • There is no known safe dose of raisins for dogs.
  • Symptoms of kidney failure may not appear for 24 to 48 hours; waiting for signs of illness can be a fatal mistake.
  • Contact an emergency veterinarian or a pet poison control center immediately after discovery.

Why should I take a photo of the raisin packaging for my vet?

  • Take a clear photo of the box and the ingredient list if you still have the packaging.
  • Sharing this with your veterinarian helps confirm the exact product type and estimate the total volume consumed.
  • Vets can check for secondary toxins like xylitol or chocolate often found in snack mixes.
  • This information is vital for determining the best course of treatment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following significant raisin ingestion in dogs, prompt decontamination is recommended, including emesis induction with 3% hydrogen peroxide (2 mL/kg, up to 45 mL) followed by activated charcoal administration. Aggressive fluid diuresis for a minimum of 48 hours is indicated in cases of large ingestions or when vomiting and/or diarrhea develop spontaneously within 12 hours of ingestion. Renal function and fluid balance must be closely monitored during fluid therapy. Asymptomatic dogs with recent ingestion exceeding 1-2 g/kg should undergo emesis induction, and activated charcoal may be beneficial for larger ingestions. Affected dogs may develop vomiting and/or diarrhea within 6-12 hours, progressing to oliguric or anuric renal failure within 24-72 hours. Other clinical signs include lethargy, anorexia, abdominal pain, weakness, dehydration, polydipsia, and tremors. Serum creatinine levels tend to rise early and disproportionately compared with serum urea nitrogen levels.

Chapter: Toxicology, Nephrology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2968)

faqs.question: How many raisins are considered dangerous for dogs?
faqs.answer: There is no established 'safe' amount. Some dogs may tolerate small amounts, while others develop life-threatening kidney failure from just one or two raisins. Always treat any ingestion as an emergency.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of raisin poisoning?
faqs.answer: Early signs include vomiting, diarrhea, and lethargy. As kidney damage progresses, you may notice increased thirst, followed by a total loss of urine production and abdominal pain.

faqs.question: Should I try to make my dog vomit at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a licensed veterinarian. Inducing vomiting incorrectly or in certain situations can lead to aspiration pneumonia or other complications.
